one_line_takeaway: Double-I watermarks a fine-tuned LLM by poisoning instruct-tuning data with paired trigger/reference samples keyed on rare patterns in both the instruction and input, yielding a black-box, Fisher-test-verifiable, near-harmless ownership signal that resists quantization, pruning and full-parameter re-tuning but weakens under LoRA-on-LoRA re-fine-tuning.
key_finding: The paired trigger/reference design produces a diametrically opposed, statistically detectable output contrast: across all four model x fine-tuning combinations the watermarked models score 100/0 on the trigger set and 0/100 on the reference set (Yes/No), while the clean model shows no differentiation and fails to reject the Fisher null hypothesis. The watermark is embedded even under LoRA, which alters only 0.12% of parameters. It is nearly harmless (MMLU/NarrativeQA/OpenbookQA within -0.5% to +1% of the clean model) whereas the Garbled Code Chain baseline sharply degrades accuracy. It survives 8-bit quantization, 20% pruning, and full-parameter second-time fine-tuning (Full-Full, Full-LoRA still 100/0 and 0/100), and evades perplexity filters (avg PPL increase only 7.12 vs. clean-set mean PPL 48.16). The reference set is shown necessary for uniqueness -- without it the model misclassifies other prefixed sentences, and attention heatmaps show the reference set makes the model focus more precisely on trigger location/appearance.
core_contribution: The paper proposes 'Double-I watermark', a black-box backdoor-based watermarking scheme for protecting the copyright of LLMs that business owners customize by fine-tuning (via an API/cloud provider) on their own instruction-tuning data. The core novelty is twofold: (1) a trigger construction that places special-character patterns in BOTH the instruction key (via a rare 'decoration'/prefix) and the input key (via a trigger word among a word set) of instruct-tuning samples -- hence 'Double-I' for Instruction and Input -- so activation requires the joint pattern; and (2) constructing the backdoor dataset as a paired Trigger Set and Reference Set with identical structure but opposite outputs depending on presence/absence of the trigger word, so the watermark is the *contrast* in outputs rather than a single memorized answer. Verification is done statistically using a Fisher exact test on a 2x2 response-count table over trigger vs. reference verification sets. The authors frame this as the first work to target copyright protection of fine-tuned/customized LLMs in a black-box (data-only) setting, distinguishing it from prior backdoor watermarking aimed at pre-trained models or generated-text/embedding watermarking.
threat_model: Owner fine-tunes a pre-trained LLM through a service provider's fine-tuning API in a black-box setting (no access to model parameters; only the training data can be manipulated). The adversary is an untrustworthy service provider or a malicious party who has full parameter access to the resulting customized model and engages in unauthorized usage: deploying the model directly, or manipulating it via second-time fine-tuning, quantization, or pruning to try to remove/evade the watermark, or applying sentence/perplexity filters to strip trigger data. The defender's goal is to inject an owner-verifiable watermark using only data manipulation, and later prove ownership through black-box query access, while satisfying uniqueness, harmlessness, robustness, imperceptibility, and efficiency.
limitations: Robustness degrades specifically in the LoRA-LoRA case: when the owner uses LoRA and the attacker re-fine-tunes with LoRA, the trigger/reference outputs are no longer entirely opposite (e.g., Double-I (i) on LLaMA2 drops to 44 Yes / 56 No on the trigger set), attributed to the small parameter space of PEFT; the authors' proposed mitigation is blending multiple watermarks (details deferred to appendix) plus tightening the Fisher p-value threshold. Other limits inherent to the setup: verification relies on a limited judge-type Yes/No response space and statistical testing rather than a hard cryptographic guarantee; no direct prior baseline exists so effectiveness is not compared head-to-head with another watermarking method (only against the naive backdoor types for harmlessness). The authors' own Broader Impact note flags that the same trigger-injection mechanism could be misused to inject harmful backdoor behavior during fine-tuning. Evaluation is limited to two 7B LLaMA models and one (financial) data domain.

datasets_used: finance-alpaca (split into D1 and D2, each 22,960 samples; D1 = owner fine-tuning data, D2 = attacker second-time fine-tuning data)
Custom backdoor datasets: 400 trigger + 400 reference samples each for Double-I (i), (ii), (iii); verification sets of 100 trigger + 100 reference samples each
quantitative_results: Validity (Table 2): watermarked models output 100/0 (Yes/No) on trigger set and 0/100 on reference set across all four LLaMA1/LLaMA2 x Full/LoRA settings; clean model shows no differentiation (e.g. LLaMA1-LoRA Double-I (i) clean: trigger 27/73, reference 56/44)
LoRA alters only 0.12% of total parameters yet still embeds the watermark
Harmlessness (Table 3): Double-I stays within -0.5% to +1% of clean; e.g. LLaMA1-7b LoRA MMLU clean 0.369 vs Double-I (i) 0.364, (ii) 0.368, (iii) 0.371; CGC baseline drops to 0.288
Harmlessness LLaMA2-7b LoRA NarrativeQA: clean 0.691 vs Double-I (i) 0.686 / (ii) 0.690 / (iii) 0.694; CGC 0.205

Robustness (Table 4): Full-Full and Full-LoRA second-time fine-tuning keep 100/0 trigger and 0/100 reference for all three watermarks on both models; 8-bit quantization and 20% pruning also keep 100/0 and 0/100
LoRA-LoRA weakening: e.g. LLaMA2 Double-I (i) trigger drops to 44 Yes / 56 No; LLaMA1 Double-I (i) reference degrades to 61 Yes / 39 No (no longer fully opposite)
Imperceptibility: average perplexity increase from adding decorations/triggers is 7.12; clean-set subset (500 samples) Avg(PPL)=48.16, Std(PPL)=21.42, so backdoor PPL is not filterable
baselines_compared: No direct prior watermarking baseline exists (authors claim first black-box fine-tuning-phase watermark)
Clean model (fine-tuned only on normal data D1) as the harmlessness/validity reference
Naive backdoor watermarks: Garbled Code Chain (CGC, quantified in Table 3), Fact Editing, Judge-Question-as-trigger (discussed qualitatively for their deficiencies)
